input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical twin is a 1/10‑scale autonomous vehicle that has been teleoperated and retrofitted with advanced sensor and control technology. Its main hardware layers consist of a lower‑level chassis, an upper‑level chassis, and autonomy elements (perception, planning, and actuation modules). The vehicle operates in a test environment for Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) experiments, featuring resource‑constrained electronic control units (ECUs) that make it a suitable candidate for off‑loading computation to a digital twin.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The digital twin delivers three core services: (1) Data Monitoring – a graphical user interface (UI) shows real‑time vehicle state, the projected driving path, and detected object tracks, and allows users to view, save, and generate reports of vehicle data; (2) Computation Offloading – the ACC algorithm (lead‑vehicle identification, desired‑speed calculation) runs on cloud resources of the twin and the resulting speed command is sent back to the vehicle for actuation; (3) Remote Management – operators can remotely enable or disable ACC, set a desired speed, and issue an emergency‑braking command via the UI. These services require low end‑to‑end latency communication and support two user classes: operators (monitoring and control) and developers (debugging and data collection).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The implementation uses a cloud‑based computation platform for ACC processing, a middleware layer that provides low‑latency bidirectional communication between the vehicle and the twin, and a web‑based UI for visualization and command entry. The overall Digital Twin System (DTS) aggregates the DT entity, physical‑twin integration components, middleware, and UI, following community best practices for safety‑critical, low‑latency operation. No specific software packages, simulation engines, or databases are identified in the provided documents.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The system requires a shared notion of time across all devices with a maximum clock offset of 100 ms, as mandated for safety‑critical vehicular applications; this accuracy is enforced by hardware‑limited synchronization mechanisms to keep physical and digital components temporally aligned.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The digital twin is decomposed into three ISO 23247 entities—Physical Twin Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and User Entity—each instantiated on a distinct hardware platform as separate ROS 2 packages; this yields a decentralized, multi‑instance architecture where coordination is performed through ROS 2 topics/services, and each entity’s scope is limited to sensor/actuator handling (Physical), data management and control logic (Digital Twin), and visualization/control interfaces (User).
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Orchestration follows the ISO 23247 reference architecture: functional entities are mapped onto ROS 2 packages (one or more nodes per package) and deployed on dedicated compute nodes; ROS 2 middleware provides inter‑entity communication and resource management, while the 100 ms time‑synchronization constraint ensures system‑wide coordination; the overall pattern is an entity‑centric, service‑oriented architecture built on ROS 2.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Within the Digital Twin system the computational workload is organized into ROS2 packages that implement Functional Entities. The Physical Twin Entity hosts a point‑cloud clustering and tracking model that processes raw sensor data. Data management is realized by a dedicated Data Storage software component, which is not aligned to a specific Functional Entity. The overall data flow connects sensors (Physical Twin Entity) → preprocessing nodes → clustering/tracking model → Digital Twin Entity for higher‑level control and monitoring.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The architecture requires that all devices share a common notion of time with a maximum synchronization error of 100 ms, dictated by hardware limitations. This time‑sync constraint is imposed to ensure consistent behavior across distributed ROS2 nodes that implement actuation and sensor data processing. No additional model validation or uncertainty quantification methods are described.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Sensor data are generated on the Physical Twin Entity by LiDAR, motor, steering‑servo and vehicle‑state units and are streamed as raw point‑cloud frames and vehicle‑state messages. The streams are first passed to the Data Translation functional entity, which serialises the data, aligns message types and performs unit conversions. Inter‑entity transport uses a Wi‑Fi network with UDP at the transport layer, while intra‑entity components communicate over a ROS2 middleware network. Within the ROS2 network the raw point‑clouds are consumed by the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking software component (part of the Data Pre‑Processing FE) where clustering, object tracking and projection of the driving path are performed before the results are forwarded to the Visualization/Presentation FE for real‑time 3‑D display. Actuation‑related data follow a similar path through the Actuation FE. Pre‑processing steps therefore include point‑cloud clustering, tracking, unit conversion and message type alignment; no explicit sampling frequency or event‑trigger description is provided in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Commands generated by the Digital Twin Entity – such as the desired speed computed by the off‑loaded ACC service, ACC enable/disable requests, and emergency‑brake signals – are packaged by the Data Translation FE (serialization, message‑type alignment and unit conversion) and transmitted back to the Physical Twin Entity over the same Wi‑Fi UDP link used for sensor upload. The command payloads conform to the vehicle’s control message format expected by the Actuation FE, where they are validated before being issued to the motor and steering actuators. Execution confirmation is implicitly provided by the actuation feedback loop that updates vehicle‑state data, which are again sent through the Data Translation FE to the DTS for monitoring. Specific authentication, conflict‑resolution or acknowledgement mechanisms are not detailed in the supplied material.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The DTS employs a two‑layer communication architecture. Internally, all software components are wired together via a ROS2 network (ROS2 DDS‑based middleware) forming the intra‑entity logical bus. For communication between the three ISO 23247‑styled entities (Physical Twin, Digital Twin, User), a Wi‑Fi network using UDP at the transport layer is used. The system requires a shared time base with a maximum clock‑skew of 100 ms to satisfy safety‑critical timing constraints. Low end‑to‑end latency is mandated for ACC operation, though exact latency or bandwidth figures are not specified. No explicit redundancy or security (encryption/authentication) protocols are mentioned in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering process follows the ISO 23247 reference architecture. In the architectural design phase the team first identifies key software components (Tab. I) and their relationships to sensors and actuators, then maps each component onto Functional Entities (FEs). Each FE is implemented as an independent ROS 2 package containing one or more ROS 2 nodes that realize the required functionality. The implementation is organized into three ISO‑defined entities: the Physical Twin Entity (resource‑specific functions such as point‑cloud clustering and tracking), the Digital Twin Entity (data‑management and control mechanisms), and the User Entity (User Interface FE split into 3D visualization and control‑panel sub‑components). The entities are deployed on separate hardware units, reflecting the ISO‑23247 notion of ownership of functionality. This mapping and deployment constitute the DT development workflow described in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The system is explicitly built on the ISO 23247 Digital Twin framework for manufacturing, using the reference architecture defined in Part 2 of the standard. Functional Entities are aligned with the ISO‑defined entities (Physical Twin Entity, Digital Twin Entity, User Entity). The implementation leverages ROS 2 packages, a widely adopted open‑source robotics middleware, to realise each FE. The document also mentions that OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA) is an example of a protocol that could be used for device coordination, indicating awareness of relevant industrial communication standards. Thus the DT complies with ISO 23247 and employs ROS 2 (and potentially OPC UA) to achieve interoperability.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The case study states that the ISO 23247 reference architecture is insufficient for safety‑critical automotive Digital Twins and recommends elevating safety to a first‑class concern by integrating established automotive safety standards, specifically ISO 26262 functional‑safety. Safety measures should be incorporated through hardware‑agnostic component designs such as AUTOSAR, enabling flexible deployment of functional elements on the physical twin. The documents do not describe any cybersecurity protocols, access‑control, authentication, or threat‑mitigation strategies.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.

_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**
block_5_docs_retrieved:
In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We

_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin

automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the

component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.

V. R EFLECTION AND C ONCLUSIONS


We reported on a case study of an automotive DT, through
which we investigate the fitness of the ISO 23247 standard
in engineering such systems. Our experiences highlight both
benefits and limitations of the standard in automotive settings.
On a positive note, we found the standard’s reference
architecture (RA) to be **a useful starting point for auto-**
**motive DTs** as it provides guidance for organizing blocks
of functionality. Additionally, we found the standard **easy to**
**use** as it does not assume substantial DT expertise and has a
comfortable learning curve. Our case was developed by two
graduate students, starting from a course project. [4] Through
this case study, we also highlight the **convenient generality**
of ISO 23247. The independence from specific format and
protocol requirements, as well as the ability to rely on domainspecific technologies in a straightforward manner, facilitated
the transfer of the RA to a new domain (automotive).
On a critical note, rigid adherence to the RA may lead to an
**under-utilization of resources** . In contrast to OMEs, vehicles
are resource-rich systems of their own. Thus, we recommend
promoting the physical twin to a first-class entity in automotive
DT architectures. This will allow for deploying FEs on the
vehicle, potentially in a flexible and dynamic fashion. For
example, our case study deploys the Data Pre-Processing FE
and the Actuation FE onto the physical twin, as opposed to
the DT Entity as prescribed by the ISO 23247 RA. Flexibility
in the deployment of FEs is at the heart of existing automotive
standards—e.g., AUTOSAR, the _de facto_ automotive software
architecture standard—where SW components are defined in a
hardware-agnostic manner, making it easier to retrofit existing
automotive architectures into automotive DT architectures.

Our case study further suggests that the RA is **insufficient for**
**safety-critical applications such as automotive** . Therefore,
we recommend safety to be promoted to a first-class citizen
in automotive DT standards by relying on existing automotive
safety standards—e.g., ISO 26262 [38].
Our work is a first look into the benefits and challenges of
adopting a standardized DT architecture for automotive DTs.
In order to aid the development of proper domain-specific
standards, we call for additional research into the topic, with
a particular emphasis on safety, security, data management,
and considering the regulated nature of the automotive domain, compatibility with existing standards. Additionally, we
encourage the development of automotive DT case studies and
exemplars to provide empirical evidence for further research.
In future work, we plan to research the safety aspects of
automotive DTs in more detail and elicit recommendations
